sanskrit_devanagari:
कर्मणैव हि संसिद्धिमास्थिता जनकादयः ।
लोकसङ्ग्रहमेवापि सम्पश्यन्कर्तुमर्हसि ॥ २० ॥
sanskrit_iast:
karmaṇaiva hi saṁsiddhim
āsthitā janakādayaḥ |
loka-saṅgraham evāpi
sampaśyan kartum arhasi || 20 ||

sanskrit_sources.agreement: exact (raw HTML scrape; akṣara sequence as published)
exact (text body identical with vedabase; punctuation rendering differs)
exact (text body identical; punctuation rendering differs)
exact (academic critical edition; cross-reference Bhg_03.020 = MBh_06,025.020). Body text identical; word-segmentation differs by edition.

translations.verbatim_quote: Kings such as Janaka attained perfection solely by performance of prescribed duties. Therefore, just for the sake of educating the people in general, you should perform your work.
(See holy-bhagavad-gita.org for full Mukundananda rendering; agreement with Prabhupada noted in disagreements section where it diverges materially.)

commentaries.verbatim_excerpt: Kings like Janaka were all self-realized souls; consequently they had no obligation to perform the prescribed duties in the Vedas. Nonetheless they performed all prescribed activities just to set examples for the people in general. Janaka was the father of Sītā and father-in-law of Lord Śrī Rāma.

commentaries.translator: Swami Gambhirananda (Advaita Ashrama edition of Sankara-bhasya, as reproduced on bhagavad-gita.us)
commentaries.verbatim_excerpt: 3.20 Hi, for; in the olden days, the leaned Ksatriyas, janakadayah, Janaka and others such as Asvapati; asthitah, strove to attain; samsiddim, Liberation; karmana eva, through action itself.
commentaries.summary_paraphrase: Shankara: even Janaka and others (kings, householders established in knowledge) reached saṁsiddhim (perfection) by karma alone — not by abandoning action. The reason is loka-saṅgraha — the holding-together of the people. The realised actor does not need karma for himself; he performs it so the people watching him are not led to abandon their own karma in confusion.

commentaries.translator: Swami Adidevananda (Sri Ramakrishna Math edition of Ramanuja's Gita-bhasya, as reproduced on bhagavad-gita.us)
commentaries.verbatim_excerpt: 3.20 It is also declared that Karma Yoga alone Janaka and others reached perfection. Because, Karma Yoga is the best means for securing the vision of the self even for a person who is qualified for Jnana Yoga, royal sages like Janaka and others, who are foremost among the Jnanins,
commentaries.summary_paraphrase: Ramanuja: Janaka and others attained perfection by performing prescribed karma without attachment, as worship of the Lord. The verse establishes the precedent: action and realisation are compatible; the realised one acts. The motivation is loka-saṅgraha — the welfare and uplift of the people — by being a visible example of right action.

positions.rendering: for the sake of educating the people in general
for the welfare of the world
loka-saṅgraha = preventing the populace from straying from the right path; the realised one acts to keep the world from confusion
loka-saṅgraha = welfare and uplift of the people; the realised one acts as model so others may be drawn toward the path

disagreements_among_translators.explanation: Both traditions read loka-saṅgraha as the second motivation for the realised actor (the first being his own remaining karma, if any). Shankara emphasises the negative function: prevent the populace from drift. Ramanuja emphasises the positive function: model the right path so others may follow. The translations split between 'educating the people' (Prabhupada, closer to Ramanuja's positive reading) and 'welfare of the world' (Mukundananda, neutral). All converge on the structural claim: the realised one's action has world-effects regardless of its self-effects.

literal_meaning: Indeed, by action alone Janaka and others attained perfection. Even with regard to loka-saṅgraha (the welfare of the world), you ought to act.
traditional_meaning_consensus: Krishna names a precedent (Janaka, the philosopher-king of Mithila) and gives the second motivation for action even after self-realisation: loka-saṅgraha — holding-the-world-together. Engagement with action is not merely instrumental for the actor's purification; it has a structural function for the social and cosmic order. This sets up the exemplar argument that runs through 3.21-3.26.
